data.title: Cleveland-Cliffs: Even Tariffs Don't Seem To Help
data.description:
If even tariffs don't help, then...

We're really pretty certain that adding tariffs to imports will increase the domestic price of that thing the tariffs have been added to. That's the purpose of them and that's what they do, these tariffs. To make foreign imports more expensive - and thus allow domestic producers to increase their prices.

This happens with steel:

That Q4 to Q1 price rise is $697 to $802.

Then:

That price rise is $594 to $629.

The added tariffs in this Q1 of 2025 have led to the European and US steel prices diverging yet more. We've had a circa 5% rise in Europe and 14% in the US. That extra increase – 9% say, just between friends – should feed through straight to the bottom line of US steel producers.

We've seen this happen too

Well, I'm going to claim it did, at least. I recommended Radius Recycling on the basis that scrap steel prices move up when finished steel does. So, a scrap steel collector should rise when steel tariffs arrive. So much so that someone came and bought the company mere weeks after I said so. My ego's not quite large enough to claim that they bought because I pointed this out but almost that large.

So, tariffs raise the price of steel within the US. And, in a leveraged manner – the price rises should feed through straight to the bottom line – to a rise in the equity prices of US steel companies.

So, why hasn't this happened?

That's the little conundrum we've got over Cleveland-Cliffs (NYSE:CLF). The tariffs should have boosted the stock price. They haven't.

There's been a little blibble along the way but nothing impressive. Yet, the selling price should have risen by 9% or 10%. And that should feed through directly to the net – not even gross, but net – margin. So, why hasn't this been transformational? A 10% of sales addition to the net profit really should make a dang difference.

One possible reason

Well, there are several possible reasons. One is simply that it's going to take time for steel-using businesses to repatriate, so there's no increase in domestic consumption as yet. But that's not quite how tariffs do work. Sure, it'll take time for that effect to happen, but prices have already moved – and as above prices feed straight through to the bottom line.

Another is that higher steel prices are impacting upon demand for domestic steel. That could be true, but I'd also be surprised if that effect is working this fast. For that would mean people are changing their production mix a great deal faster than we've already assumed they will in that paragraph above.

A third would be that we're seeing here the beginnings of a recession. Steel orders do drop off before we all realize we're in a recession because it's durable goods orders that tend to drop off first. We only know – in fact, we only define it as – we're in a recession when all production starts to fall, and that's a little later in the cycle. And, of course, not all falls in durable goods orders lead to a recession.

The financials

Harrison Schwartz has an excellent piece on the numbers at Cleveland-Cliffs. Rather than my repeating all of that, just read him.

My major point here is different. I'm taking, perhaps, a 30,000-foot view. Or, perhaps, not a financial markets view but a metals industry one.

Another way to put this is that I'm interested in the structural problem here, not that of the passing day.

Imports never have been the steel problem

Cleveland-Cliffs is – now at least – what we call an integrated steel producer. They mine ore – that's the origin of the business – and they process through blast furnaces, BOFs, produce steel then roll for the auto business and ship. As an example of one – major – line of business. From a hole in the ground to something being shipped to auto manufacturers – integrated.

Great, fine, and lovely – and many fortunes have been made over the centuries by doing just that. The thing is that every technology has its day and when that day is done, well, it's done. It's possible to make money running a declining technology, sure. Fortunes have been made that way too. But it requires a rigorous attention to costs, a refusal to try to expand and basically running every piece of equipment until it falls apart then not replacing it. Which isn't how it's being done here. CLF has expanded, is still carrying the debt from having expanded recently, and has at least been making noises about expanding again by taking over US Steel.

Now, we all know that US steel (small s, not US Steel) has had problems in recent decades. Furnaces closing all over the country, vast combines going bust, and all that. A lot of the blame has been put on steel imports. Used to be the Japanese everyone complained about now it's the Chinese. But some of them, some others, stealing our good union jobs and so on. We've had varied bursts of tariffs along the way too.

But the basic problem has continued. Because imports haven't been the actual problem. Technological change has been.

Some fun numbers

I love the US Geological Survey for they produce all the useful statistics in the metals world. In fact, I once started a business off the back of one single telephone conversation with one of their analysts. That business made me a living for 15 years, too – I love USGS. And here is the spreadsheet of their historical steel production/consumption numbers for the US.

Just before Covid (to be reasonable) apparent consumption was some 100 million tonnes. Which is about 1966 and 1968 numbers. Yes, imports are up by perhaps 18 million tonnes. But then so are exports up by 6 million tonnes. So, trade makes a difference of say 12 million tonnes to domestic production. And note that in the intervening years, there's – sometimes at least – been much higher US consumption.

However, Nucor was really only just starting out back in the mid-60s. Nucor is the largest US steelmaker these days; 18 million tonnes, apparently. 18 is bigger than 12 note.

Nucor only, exclusively, recycles scrap steel into new steel. No blast furnaces at all. And, get this, Nucor is not the only company that does this within the US.

The big problem for those integrated steelmakers is not, in fact, trade. It's technological change.

Agreed, this is about another country but in the UK only 30% of steel is now made in blast furnaces/BOFs. The direction of travel is certain.

There's a reason for this too

Essentially, when you build out a civilisation for the first time – as China is doing, for example, then you've got to use a great deal of steel. But you've not got any. Therefore, you use a great deal of iron ore to make steel. OK, seems simple enough.

But when you're building Civilisation 2.0, or 3.0, then you tear down the old version and that means you've old steel that you can use to build the next version. And, as these things happen, we tend to use less steel in each subsequent version – that's why the US population has doubled since the 1960s, the economy at least doubled per capita – so a 4x economy – but we're still consuming the same amount of steel, that 100 million tonnes or so.

Of that 100 million tonnes, a great deal – I've seen guesses at 55% or so – is coming from scrap recycled through an electric arc furnace, not made anew from iron ore.

This is clearly a problem for an industry that was set up to produce that 100 million tonnes from ore back those 60 years. Half that market has disappeared – whatever we think or talk about imports.

Therefore, limiting imports isn't the solution to the problem.

This is what I think the Cleveland-Cliffs problem is

They're an integrated steel producer, from ore mines to rolling mills, when we simply don't want, need nor require as much integrated capacity as we've got.

It's not about trade – or, at best, it's only very partially about trade. It's really technological change. And my expectation is that the US capacity for virgin steel production ("virgin" here means from ore) is going to continue to decline as it has been for decades now. This does mean that more of the US's current capacity for making virgin steel is going to close in the coming years. And, as is usual in such cases, it's going to be the financially weakest parts of the industry that go under.

Why I'm wrong

Now, of course, error is always possible. Perhaps tariffs are going to become so terrifying that no one ever buys a foreign product made using steel at all. Therefore, domestic demand for steel will indeed rise. Rise so far that the scrap feed is insufficient and blast furnaces rule again.

If that happens, then it's going to take some time, obviously. However, it could happen.

My opinion

I'm not saying that Cleveland-Cliffs is going to fall over next week. I'm not even saying that it will fall over. Rather, I'm trying to answer a much more specific question.

We'd expect tariffs to improve the stock price of those who produce, domestically, what has just been tariffed. This hasn't happened. Why?

Because it's not, in fact, international trade that has been causing the problems. Rather, it's technological change. This means that a change in tariffs – which affect trade – doesn't solve the problem.

I am wholly convinced that more of US primary steel production is going to disappear in the coming years. Whether it will be Cleveland-Cliffs or some other that does is a different matter. But this essential structural problem is, to me, why tariffs haven't boosted the CLF price. And, in fact, won't.

Returns represent past performance and do not guarantee future results. Investment return and share price will fluctuate with market conditions, and investors may have a gain or loss when shares are sold. Mutual Fund performance changes over time and currently may be significantly lower than stated above. Performance is updated and published monthly. Current month-end performance can be obtained atdodgeandcox.com or by calling 800-621-3979.

Market Commentary

Markets were volatile in the first quarter of the year, influenced by uncertainty surrounding the Trump administration’s policies, particularly regarding tariffs and their implications for growth and inflation. Although the U.S. economy has generally remained resilient, pockets of weakness have recently emerged, especially in consumer spending and sentiment. Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve held its benchmark rate steady as Fed Chair Jerome Powell indicated policymakers needed “greater clarity” before making adjustments. The central bank’s economic projections suggested slower growth and higher core inflation by year-end.

U.S. equity markets were challenged in this environment, particularly the “Magnificent Seven” stocks, which had their worst quarterly performance in two years. Consequently, the S&P 500 Index returned -4.3% and the valuation spread between value and growth stocks narrowed.

Within bond markets, long-term U.S. Treasury yields declined and credit spreads widened modestly, reflecting shifting investor sentiment. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index returned 2.8% for the quarter.

Portfolio Strategy

As market pricing shifted over the quarter, we adhered to our disciplined and long-term investment approach, making gradual adjustments based on our outlook for the Fund’s equity and fixed income securities. Despite these changes, the Fund’s asset allocation as of March 31 remains thematically the same: 47.4% in U.S. equities, 17.7% in non-U.S. equities, and 34.9% in fixed income securities plus cash.[2]

Within the equity portion of the Fund, three major themes have persisted over the past several quarters:

  1. Intentional exposure to companies in less cyclical sectors, such as Consumer, Health Care, Utilities, and Real Estate. We believe these Fund holdings represent durable franchises that can navigate various macroeconomic circumstances and thus provide ballast from a risk management perspective.
  2. Sizeable exposure to individually selected international equities, offering diversification benefits as well as attractive relative value and return expectations.
  3. Utilizing S&P 500 futures to reduce the Fund’s aggregate equity market exposure given elevated and unattractive valuations while staying invested in the specific equity securities we find attractive on a bottom-up basis.

Within the fixed income segment of the Fund, we continue to maintain considerable exposures to Credit and Agency[3] mortgage-backed securities (MBS) sectors. We believe the portfolio’s carefully selected credit securities are fundamentally sound, diversified across multiple dimensions, and offer attractive long-term return potential. We find the Fund’s Agency MBS holdings compelling because of low prepayment rates and attractive valuations.

Recent Market Volatility

The Trump administration's tariff policy rollout in the first weeks of April increased fears of higher inflation and a possible recession, which led to increased market volatility. When market prices change, we revisit our investment theses security-by-security, incorporate recent developments, reassess our long-term outlook, and make incremental changes to the portfolio. We’ve navigated challenging economic periods in the past and have used them to uncover attractive long-term investment opportunities at compelling valuations. Our past experiences give us confidence in our investment approach and ability to serve our shareholders' long-term best interests through the market’s current turbulence.

We remain optimistic about the long-term prospects for the Fund, which is diversified across a broad range of sectors and investment themes.

We encourage shareholders to take a long-term view and are here to answer any questions you may have.

Thank you for the continued confidence you’ve placed in Dodge & Cox.

Performance Review (Fund’s Class I Shares)

First Quarter

Equity sector allocation and security selection were the main drivers of the Fund’s relative outperformance.

Equity Portfolio (vs. S&P 500)

Key contributors to relative results included the portfolio's:

  • Overweight position and holdings in Health Care, particularly CVS Health;
  • Information Technology underweight position and stock selection;
  • Overweight position and stock selection in Financials; and
  • Stock selection and underweight position in Consumer Discretionary, including overweight position in Alibaba.

Key detractors from relative results included the portfolio's:

  • Position in Avantor (AVTR).

Fixed Income Portfolio (vs. Bloomberg U.S. Agg)

Key contributors to relative results included the portfolio's:

  • Credit security selection, most notably Citigroup, Southern Company (SO), British American Tobacco, and TC Energy (TRP).

Key detractors from relative results included the portfolio’s:

  • Below-benchmark duration position; and
  • Underweight position in U.S. Treasuries and overweight position in corporate bonds.

This agreement cannot be terminated prior to April 30, 2026 other than by resolution of the Fund’s Board of Trustees. For purposes of the foregoing, ordinary expenses shall not include nonrecurring shareholder account fees, fees and expenses associated with Fund shareholder meetings, fees on portfolio transactions such as exchange fees, dividends and interest on short positions, fees and expenses of pooled investment vehicles that are held by the Fund, interest expenses and other fees and expenses related to any borrowings, taxes, brokerage fees and commissions and other costs and expenses relating to the acquisition and disposition of Fund investments, other expenditures which are capitalized in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles, and other non-routine expenses or extraordinary expenses not incurred in the ordinary course of the Fund’s business, such as litigation expenses. The term of the agreement will automatically renew for subsequent three-year terms unless terminated with at least 30 days’ written notice by either party prior to the end of the then-current term. The agreement does not permit Dodge & Cox to recoup any fees waived or payments made to the Fund for a prior year.

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact concerning any market, industry or investment. Data has been obtained from sources considered reliable, but Dodge & Cox makes no representations as to the completeness or accuracy of such information. The information provided is historical and does not predict future results or profitability. This is not a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any security and is not indicative of Dodge & Cox’s current or future trading activity. Any securities identified are subject to change without notice and do not represent a Fund’s entire holdings. Dodge & Cox does not guarantee the future performance of any account (including Dodge & Cox Funds) or any specific level of performance, the success of any investment decision or strategy that Dodge & Cox may use, or the success of Dodge & Cox’s overall management of an account.

The Fund invests in individual stocks, bonds and other securities whose market values fluctuate within a wide range, so that your investment may be worth more or less than its original cost. The Fund’s performance could be hurt by equity risk, market risk, manager risk, liquidity risk, geographic risk and derivatives risk. In addition, the Fund’s fixed income performance could be hurt by interest rate risk, credit risk, below-investment grade securities risk, mortgage- and asset-backed securities risk, to-be- announced transaction risk, and call risk. The Fund may use derivatives to create or hedge investment exposure, which may involve additional and/or greater risks than investing in securities, including more liquidity risk and the risk of a counterparty default. Some derivatives create leverage.

Before investing in any Dodge & Cox Fund, you should carefully consider the Fund’s investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses. To obtain a Fund’s prospectus and summary prospectus, which contain this and other important information, or for current month-end performance figures, visit dodgeandcox.com or call 800- 621-3979. Please read the prospectus and summary prospectus carefully before investing.

Dodge & Cox Funds are distributed by Foreside Fund Services, LLC, which is not affiliated with Dodge & Cox.

The S&P 500 Index is a product of S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC and/or its affiliates and has been licensed for use by Dodge & Cox. Copyright 2024, S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global, Inc., and/ or its affiliates. FTSE Russell is a trading name of certain of the LSE Group companies. “Russell®” is/ are a trade mark(s) of the relevant LSE Group companies and is used by any other LSE Group company under license.

BLOOMBERG® and the Bloomberg indices listed herein are service marks of Bloomberg Finance L.P. and its affiliates. Bloomberg is not affiliated with Dodge & Cox and has not reviewed or approved any data or information used herein.

Footnotes

  1. All returns are stated in U.S. dollars, unless otherwise noted. The Funds’ total returns include the reinvestment of dividend and capital gain distributions, but have not been adjusted for any income taxes payable by shareholders on these distributions or on Fund share redemptions. Index returns include dividend and/or interest income but, unlike Fund returns, do not reflect fees or expenses. The Class X shares inception date is May 2, 2022. The returns shown prior to that date are for the Class I shares. The Combined Index reflects an unmanaged portfolio (rebalanced monthly) of 60% of the S&P 500 Index, which is a market capitalization-weighted index of 500 large-capitalization stocks commonly used to represent the U.S. equity market, and 40% of the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (Bloomberg U.S. Agg), which is a widely recognized, unmanaged index of U.S. dollar- denominated, investment-grade, taxable fixed income securities. The Fund may, however, invest up to 75% of its total assets in equity securities.
  2. Unless otherwise specified, all weightings and characteristics are as of March 31, 2025.
  3. The U.S. Government does not guarantee the Fund’s shares, yield, or net asset value.
  4. The agency guarantee (by, for example, Ginnie Mae, Fannie Mae, or Freddie Mac) does not eliminate market risk.

See Disclosures for a full list of financial terms and Index definitions.

Original Post

data.title: FS KKR Capital: How Good Is This 15% Yield?
data.description:
Article Thesis

FS KKR Capital Corp (NYSE:FSK) is a Business Development Company that has recently seen its shares come under pressure, which made its dividend yield rise from an already high level to an even higher level of 15%. There is some dividend reduction risk when investing in FS KKR Capital, but even if the dividend were to be reduced, the yield would likely remain quite high. Shares are now trading at a pretty inexpensive valuation, suggesting right now is a better-than-average time to enter or expand a position in this high-yielding BDC.

Past Coverage

I have written about FS KKR Capital Corp here on Seeking Alpha in the past, with my previous article being from December 2024. I gave the company a "Buy" rating back then -- shares are down slightly since then but have outperformed the broad market since that article was published, thanks, in part, to FS KKR's attractive dividend yield. With FS KKR Capital having reported its Q4 and FY 2024 results since my last article was published, and with the stock's valuation declining, it is time to take another look at this company today.

FS KKR Capital: Recent Performance

We recently got FS KKR Capital's earnings results for the fourth quarter and for fiscal 2024, which we can use to gauge the company's recent operational performance. While results beat estimates, they weren't great in absolute terms, but that wasn't a surprise.

The company recorded net investment income per share of $0.61, down from $0.71 per share one year earlier. Adjusted net investment income per share, which backs out times that can hurt the comparability between two quarters, came in at $0.66 per share, down from $0.75 per share one year earlier. This represents a decline of 12% over the last year -- not a disaster, but not great, either. In some cases, Business Development Companies report declining net investment income per share when their share count rises due to new shares being issued. This wasn't the case here, however, as FS KKR Capital's share count remained unchanged over the last year, at 280 million.

Instead, FS KKR Capital experienced a decline in its total assets as well as in its assets at fair value, which dropped by around $1.2 billion, respectively, over the last year. This pencils out to a decline rate of a little less than 10%. The decline rate in FS KKR Capital's assets thus was a little smaller than the decline rate in the company's per-share net investment income, which can be explained by operating leverage working against the company, which made its profits decline a little more than its asset base.

Interest rates also were a bit of a headwind for the company. The following chart shows the Federal Reserve's effective federal funds rate:

We see that rates rose tremendously in 2020 and the first half of 2023, but they stagnated for much of 2024 and began to decline towards the end of last year. The federal funds rate was thus lower in Q4 of 2024 compared to Q4 of 2023, which explains the following line from FS KKR Capital's most recent earnings presentation:

Note: The data is, from left to right, for Q4 2023, Q1 2024, Q2 2024, Q3 2024, and Q4 2024

We see that the weighted average annual yield on debt investments dropped from a little more than 12% to around 11% as of the end of the most recent quarter -- 11% is still a quite high interest rate, of course, but nevertheless below the previous year's level. Like most BDCs, FS KKR Capital makes a lot of floating-rate loans, so when interest rates head lower, that is reflected in the company's results.

The good news is that the Federal Reserve has not lowered rates further so far this year. Thus, if things stay as they are right now, the yield on FS KKR Capital's debt investments should remain at the current level and not sink further. Of course, if the US were to get into a recession, the Federal Reserve may lower interest rates, but a recession is far from a sure thing, I believe.

With interest rates stabilizing, at least for now, that should cease to be a headwind soon. There is, of course, still the headwind of a shrinking portfolio -- although we do not know how long this will remain in place. For the last couple of quarters, there were not only declines on a year-over-year basis but also on a quarter-to-quarter basis -- I thus do not believe that the shrinking will stop immediately. On the other hand, FS KKR has a strong brand (via KKR (KKR)); thus, there is potential for increased deal activity. Therefore, I don't believe that we will see the portfolio shrink forever, although we may see a couple of quarters of declines.

Portfolio Quality And Risks

Despite the declining overall portfolio value, the number of portfolio companies has increased over the last year, rising from 204 to 214. FS KKR Capital has thus become somewhat more diversified over the last year, which generally is a good thing. Around two-thirds of FS KKR Capital's assets consist of senior secured debt, with the remainder being riskier assets such as subordinated debt, preferred equity, and so on.

The ratio of investments that are on non-accrual status has risen, unfortunately, now standing at 2.2% of fair value. While that is not ultra-high, it is higher compared to some higher-quality BDCs, and the trend is in the wrong direction -- ideally, we would want to see a declining non-accrual rate. With uncertainties rising due to the recent tariff news, which could be a problem for some smaller companies, it is possible, although not guaranteed, that the non-accrual ratio will climb further during Q1 and Q2 of this year.

Valuation And Dividend

Many BDCs offer high dividend yields, but FS KKR Capital offers an especially high dividend. Accounting for the $0.64 per share per quarter base dividend and the $0.06 per share per quarter supplemental dividend, FS KKR Capital pays out $2.80 per year in dividends, which translates into a dividend yield of 14.6% at current prices.

Of course, a dividend yield this high suggests that a dividend cut would not be a large surprise. And based on the fact that adjusted net investment income didn't cover the dividend during the most recent quarter -- $0.66 per share versus $0.70 per share -- a dividend reduction would indeed make some sense.

The good news is that even a 20% or 30% dividend reduction would not result in a low dividend yield at all -- if FS KKR Capital were to cut the dividend by 20%, the yield would drop to 11.7%, while a 30% reduction would result in a dividend yield of a little north of 10%. For someone buying right now, even a sizable dividend cut by up to 30% would thus not result in a single-digit dividend yield -- the yield would remain in the double-digits.

The recent share price drop has made FS KKR Capital's valuation decline as well: The BDC's book value per share stands at 23.64 as of the end of 2024, meaning shares now trade at 0.81x book value. Relative to how FS KKR Capital traded in the recent past, that is a relatively attractive valuation, as we can see in the following chart:

Right now could thus be a better-than-average time to buy into FS KKR Capital for those who are interested in this high-yielding BDC, but others may want to wait for the current uncertainties to wane before deploying money into a BDC like FSK.

data.title: General Motors Could Gain From Tesla Backlash, But Tariffs Create Uncertainty
data.description:
General Motors (NYSE:GM) was able to beat the EPS and revenue estimates in the previous earnings call. But the stock has not seen an improvement in sentiment. In YTD the stock has declined by 12%. The recent tariffs and economic headwinds can further reduce the sentiment towards the stock. GM has a very diverse supply chain, and it will be hurt if the tariffs are finalized at the rate mentioned earlier in April. However, recent news has reported that the White House might pause tariffs on autos. Besides tariffs, an economic slowdown will also hurt GM's margins. On the positive side, GM is reporting rapid improvement in EV market share as Tesla’s (TSLA) market share continues to decline. This trend can increase as Tesla’s brand backlash intensifies.

GM has also ended its Cruise robotaxi business, in which the company invested $10 billion since acquiring it in 2016. This can improve the margins, and GM could still use some of the lessons in its advanced driver assistance system. GM is also making huge buybacks, which are helping the EPS growth trajectory. The forward PE ratio of GM for the fiscal year ending Dec 2025 is 4.07, but there is a huge gap in the EPS estimate. This shows the uncertainty in earnings faced by the company. Due to the mix of these challenges and positive tailwinds, I rate the stock a Hold at the current price.

A possible EV winner

GM’s EV market in U.S. in Q4 2024 was 12.5% according to company estimates. This was a big jump from 6.5% in Q1 2024. GM is now in the second position behind Tesla. We have already seen Tesla report weak deliveries due to customer backlash towards its brand. According to market share calculation by Caredge, GM had an EV market share of a mere 0.3% in Q1 2022. This has increased to 12.6% by Q4 2024. This is quite a big achievement for the company. On the other hand, Tesla’s market share has dropped from 74.8% in Q1 2022 to 44.4% in Q4 2024. We could see this trend continue over the next few quarters as GM improves its product line and becomes an alternative choice to Tesla.

Figure: Growth in GM’s EV market share in last few quarters. Source: Caredge, Cox Automotive

Figure: Growth in GM’s market share in last few quarters. Source: Company Filings

Tesla recently reported deliveries of 336,681 in the first quarter. This was equal to a 13% YoY decline, and it is bigger than the decline estimated by most analysts. GM has the brand strength and good product lineup to grab customers who might be looking for an alternative EV option.

Source: Decline in Tesla's deliveries. Source: Tesla Filings

GM’s good performance in gaining EV market share can convince Wall Street about the product lineup of the company and the effectiveness of its EV strategy.

Tariff and economic headwinds

GM is now facing massive headwinds due to the recent tariff announcement. We are seeing continuous changes in tariff rates, and the final decision on tariffs might take a few weeks. Recently, it was mentioned that auto tariffs might be removed. However, we would still need to see how the tariff situation evolves. It is likely that GM will face some supply chain pressure due to its diverse manufacturing operations.

Figure: GM sales in different countries. Source: Company Filings

GM has a massive presence in China, where it was already facing pricing pressure from domestic brands. This could increase as the geopolitical rhetoric between the U.S. and China could lead to more customers using domestic brands in China. Another big challenge for GM is a possible economic slowdown or even a recession. According to CNBC, more than 60% of U.S. CEOs expect a recession in the next 6 months. Tariff uncertainty can lead to a delay in capex by companies, which can lead to further economic slowdown. This causes a vicious cycle, like we saw during the Great Recession in 2008. GM is a cyclical stock and depends on good economic conditions to deliver healthy profits. Any dip in customer sentiment can lead to a big headwind for GM.

Cruise and buybacks

GM has ended its Cruise robotaxi business after investing close to $10 billion in this unit since acquiring it in 2016. I believe it was a wrong decision to compete with Google’s (GOOG)(GOOGL) Waymo in the robotaxi business. Big tech companies like Google have massive resources, and they can wait a long time to monetize new products. The EBIT impact of Cruise in calendar year 2023 was negative $2.7 billion, and in calendar year 2024 it was negative $1.7 billion. These losses were 20% of the total EBIT for CY 2023 and another 12% for CY 2024.

Figure: EBIT breakdown of GM. Source: Company Filings

GM has closed its robotaxi business, which should help in improving its EBIT and EPS in 2025. GM will now use its autonomous driving skills in its advanced driver assistance system. This could be a big win for GM, which can differentiate the company from many competitors. We could see better monetization of this service and improvement of the margins for the company.

GM is also making massive buybacks. This will reduce its outstanding stock and improve its EPS. The forward PE multiple is only 4.07, which should allow the company to reduce its share count significantly.

GM's outstanding shares have declined to 1.075 billion in the recent quarter, compared to almost 1.45 billion a few quarters back. We could see a continuation of this trend as the company invests more resources in reducing its shares while the PE multiple is low.

Low PE multiple, but still a Hold rating

Despite the attractive PE multiple, GM is facing a number of challenges, which are mentioned above. The stock has not been a good performer in the last decade. It will take at least another quarter to see if the company can deliver on its EV promise and the impact of the tariff war on the financials. This uncertainty is also reflected in the forward EPS estimate of GM.

For the fiscal year ending Dec 2025, the lowest EPS estimate is $7.85 while the highest is $12.5. For the fiscal year ending Dec 2026, this divergence is even bigger, with the lowest EPS estimate of $6.82 and the highest EPS estimate of $16.06. Hence, the highest EPS estimate is 2.35 times the lowest EPS estimate. For a mature company like GM, this is a big gap. Even tech companies like Google have a lower EPS estimate gap, which shows greater certainty in their forward earnings and a lower risk for the stock.

Figure: Forward EPS estimate of GM in the next two years. Source: Seeking Alpha

Investor Takeaway

GM stock has seen a correction of over 10% in YTD. It is facing tariff uncertainty. Despite a recent announcement of a tariff pause on autos, we could still see a margin decline in GM as the supply chain gets negatively impacted. On the other hand, GM has performed well in the EV segment and is now rapidly gaining market share. GM had 12.5% EV market share in Q4 2024, which places the company right behind Tesla. If Tesla continues to see a market share decline, GM could gain a better market share in the next few quarters.

GM has also closed its Cruise robotaxi business, which was causing massive losses. This should improve the margins for the company. A strong buyback pace will also help GM in reducing the share count. While the company has a low forward PE multiple, the EPS estimates show huge divergence, which makes the stock a bit more risky. It would be ideal to wait another quarter to see a clear picture of the tariffs and the performance of the company. This has led to a Hold rating for the stock at the current price.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Nvidia To Take $5.5B Hit On H20 Chip
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

U.S. government informs Nvidia (NVDA) it requires license to export H20 hardware to China. (00:22) Bagged and dragged: Vera Bradley (VRA) sets new lows as rebranding falls flat. (01:39) Major shareholder seeks Harley-Davidson (HOG) board shakeup, calls for CEO to step down - report. (03:00)

This is an abridged transcript.

Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) received a notice from the U.S. government that it needs a special license to ship its H20 graphic processing units or related hardware to China, and such a license will be required indefinitely.

Due to this development, Nvidia expects to take a charge of $5.5B in its upcoming quarterly financial results, according to a Form 8-K filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission.

Nvidia shares are down 6% in early pre-market trading.

Nvidia declined to comment on the issue.

Although Nvidia's more advanced artificial intelligence GPUs have already been banned for export to China, the H20 chip was designed specifically to comply with export restrictions.

The H20 is the most advanced chip offered by Nvidia that was still allowed for export to China. Industry observers and Chinese tech companies expected the H20 to be added to the list of other banned hardware. Last week, a report surfaced that the Trump Administration was pausing the implementation of an export ban to China for Nvidia's H20 GPUs.

Alibaba (BABA), Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY)(OTCPK:TCTZF) and ByteDance (BDNCE) placed at least $16B in orders for Nvidia's H20 server chips in the first three months of 2025, ahead of a potential U.S. ban on the sale of the AI chip in China.

Vera Bradley (NASDAQ:VRA) extended its losing streak with a fourth straight day in the red and another all-time low as the company’s efforts to rebrand and transition to e-commerce from brick-and-mortar are falling flat with investors.

Shares slipped below $2 on Tuesday. VRA was up 5% postmarket, although still below the $2 mark.

Since reporting Q4 results last month, the stock has dropped 30% versus a 5% drop in the S&P 500 during the same time frame.

The company is also taking more aggressive actions to reduce its reliance on clearance and liquidation activities, optimize inventory management to include the ability to rapidly adjust to evolving customer preferences, and diversify distribution through greater engagement with Target (TGT) and other major retailers.

Despite these efforts, and with the added benefit of no debt, $30M in cash and $75M in liquidity, investors continue to stay away from the stock.

“There is no guarantee that Vera can return to the profitability it enjoyed in the past. The company has damaged the brand power of its namesake flagship by doing the rebrand,” Seeking Alpha analyst group Quipus Capital said earlier this year.

Investment firm H Partners—which owns about a 9% stake in Harley-Davidson (NYSE:HOG)—is preparing for a boardroom battle to remove three directors from Harley-Davidson's eight-member board at the annual meeting on May 14.

The Wall Street Journal reported, citing people familiar with the matter that the targeted directors include CEO Jochen Zeitz, who has been on the board for 18 years and became CEO in 2020, Thomas Linebarger (17 years on the board), and Sara Levinson (29 years on the board).

The investment firm is running a withhold-the-vote campaign to push them out amid concerns over the company's declining sales and a 45% drop in its share price over the past year.

The firm’s former board member Jared Dourdeville recently resigned, criticizing Harley-Davidson (NYSE:HOG) for "cultural depletion" linked to senior leadership departures and remote work policies.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

United beats FQ1 estimates but issues profit warning if economy stalls

AST SpaceMobile seesaws amid takeover, contract speculation

Trump says U.S. raking in ‘record numbers’ from tariffs, points to lower inflation

Catalyst watch:

  • Subaru (OTCPK:FUJHY), Hyundai (OTCPK:HYMTF), Maserati (STLA), Toyota (TM), and AB Volvo's (OTCPK:VOLAF) Mack Trucks will hold press events at the New York International Auto Show.

  • DocuSign (DOCU) will hold its Momentum NYC event. CEO Allan Thygesen will give a keynote speech. Last year, DocuSign rallied after it showcased its new platform at the event.

  • Daktronics (DAKT) will hold a special shareholder meeting to vote on a reincorporation proposal and other matters. The reincorporation vote is part of a broader agreement between Daktronics and Alta Fox, which included a standstill agreement and Alta Fox agreeing to support the move to Delaware from South Dakota.

  • Ford Motor (F) Vice Chair John Lawler will participate in the Bank of America Securities Auto Summit at 9:40 am. Lawler will join analyst John Murphy for a fireside chat to discuss how the Ford+ plan is remaking Ford into a higher growth, higher margin, more capital efficient company.

  • Rivian Automotive (RIVN) CFO Claire McDonough will participate in the Bank of America Securities 2025 Automotive Summit.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in mixed territory. Crude oil is up 0.8% at $61/barrel. Bitcoin is flat at $83,000. Gold is up 1.7% at $3,306.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is down 0.4% and the DAX is down 0.3%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Interactive Brokers Group (NASDAQ:IBKR) -9% – Shares slumped after the company delivered mixed Q1 results, with earnings falling short of expectations despite a 28% dividend hike and the announcement of a 4-for-1 stock split.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Retail Sales

  • 9:15 am Industrial Production

  • 1:30 pm Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell will speak on the economic outlook before the Economic Club of Chicago.

  • 7:00 pm Kansas City Federal Reserve Bank President Jeffrey Schmid will speak on the economy and community banking with Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas President Lorie Logan before a Global Perspectives event hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas and the Dallas Citizens Council.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JD Sports: Not Able To Outrun Tariff Uncertainty
data.description:
JD Sports (OTCPK:JDDSF) (OTCPK:JDSPY) stock popped last week with a relief rally on the back of a relieving FY25 trading update. The shares finished the week up a stunning 14.9%, but are still down an eye-watering 23.4% YTD, and are 68.8% off their all-time high. Questions remain as to whether JD Sports stock can ride higher, but tariffs are a massive worry for the footwear and apparel retailer.

Not a Likeable Scenario

After numerous downgrades to its guidance in FY25, which led to the JD Sports share price retrenching in value throughout the year, the bleeding looks to have stopped after management reiterated its January outlook. It said that trading in Q4 had been in line with expectations, and that it can expect organic sales growth of 5.8% for FY25, with an and adjusted PBT of £915-935MM.

As a whole, the company saw LFL sales grow by a mere 0.3%, as performance in sports retail was bogged down by poor consumer confidence and tough comps throughout the year. Nonetheless, organic sales did tick up by 5.8% thanks to numerous acquisitions in the US and Europe, which expanded JD's store space.

All in all, the update was seen as a massive win, judging by the share price reaction, as there had been fears building up that CEO Régis Schultz would've guided lower and disappoint investors yet again. That being said, investors look to have only processed the short term, with the longer-term outlook not giving any reason to fully rejoice.

This is because guidance for the year ahead was relatively flat. Whilst revenue is estimated to grow c.10.0%, this will likely be driven by the expanding store footprint, as LFL sales is anticipated to turn negative. Consequently, the team has guided for adjusted PBT of c.£920MM, which amounts to effectively zero growth － and that's before potential tariffs are even taken into consideration.

Therefore, this means that FY26 could very well be a repeat of FY25, where profit guidance is consistently downgraded throughout the year. The higher opex from the bigger store footprint, labour cost inflation, and heftier IT investments, when/if combined with tariffs, will easily offset any cost savings and scale efficiencies JD has touted.

A Slowdown in Pace

JD does have somewhat of a margin cushion, nonetheless, as it has better pricing power as compared to its competitors, with its position on the premium end of the market. This has allowed its adjusted gross margin to retain a relatively robust c.47.8% in FY25, in spite of the acquisitions of Hibbett and Courir, which diluted gross margin by 19bps in H1.

On that basis, the outlook for trading remains extremely volatile. Along with the higher ups, we're unconvinced about the prospects in JD's home market in the UK. Not only will the hikes in employers' national insurance and the minimum living wage pile on c.£30MM of additional costs, consumer confidence and retail spending also remain depressed and below 2023 levels.

Making matters worse, JD even downgraded its medium-term outlook for the sports fashion market, expecting growth rates to be much slower than initially forecasted (c.2.5% p.a. for 2024-30 from c.8.0% for 2022-27). Thus, the firm will have to ensure that it strikes a healthy balance between sales and margins via cost efficiencies from economies of scale with its recent acquisitions.

Be that as it may, much of the gloom had already been priced in anyway, but the biggest worry in the near term is the impact of larger tariffs. President Trump may have paused reciprocal tariffs for 90 days, which has led to markets rebounding, but investors should still be aware that it's a pause, and that those hefty levies could very well come back into place without changes.

Considering that the bulk of JD's revenue now comes from the US, where it heavily relies on sports brands like NIKE (NKE), this is a huge concern. Keep in mind that many of the sports goods JD sells are manufactured in countries like Vietnam, Thailand, and China. If the Trump administration chooses to reinstate levies of 40%+, it would make JD's margin cushion disappear overnight.

Tweaking the Game Plan

Aside from that, it doesn't help that Trump's tariffs could spark a global trade war and trigger a recession into motion, too. After all, growth in the footwear and sports apparel market has been depressed over the past year or so. In fact, Nike has been struggling with a huge overhang of stock because of this, as demand has waned.

Hence, given the inconsistencies surrounding Trump's tariff policy, it's no surprise to see the board state that "the outcome of these developments is uncertain". However, the hope here is that the aforementioned countries will come to the negotiating table before the 90 days are up. This way, a much lower tariff rate can come into play, with Vietnam already in negotiations.

Even so, it's worth noting that a 10.0% tariff is still currently in place for all nations, and if continued to be upheld, would still put downward pressure on JD's margins. Nevertheless, Trump has expressed some openness in removing the minimum 10.0% levy if "it's good for both parties", in response to a recent question about whether countries can negotiate to bring it down.

"It depends on what they're adding. Some counties have big surpluses with us, and others, it's not that way, so it depends. That's why every country is different."

President Donald Trump

Having said that, it's clear that JD will have to tweak its game plan if it wants to retain its investment case. As such, we appreciate the group shifting its strategy towards cash generation and shareholder returns, rather than store expansion. But thankfully, even taking the lowest range from current consensus estimates, there still looks to be substantial upside to be realised.

But as we don't cover the stock, we refrain from issuing an official rating. That said, our model suggests upside of c.88.1%, with a forward dividend yield of c.1.3%. This is derived by comparing JD Sports's key ratios to their historical and sector(s) averages, before averaging their resulting fair values altogether. Although, we stress that this is contingent on consensus estimates.

Price Model JD Sports Sector AVG 5Y AVG Fair Value
EV/Adjusted EBITDA (10% wt) 3.9 13.1 10.7 223p
P/E (10% wt) 5.9 23.6 29.3 322p
FP/E (25% wt) 6.6 24.1 16.4 174p
PEG (55% wt) 2.9 2.4 1.4 50p
Fair Value 137p

***As a reminder to our readers, JD Sports's primary listing is on the London Stock Exchange (LSE:JD). Trading this stock on OTCMKTS may present liquidity risks, resulting in higher bid-ask spreads. For optimal execution and more accurate price discovery, we recommend assessing the stock on the LSE instead, where possible.

Explaining Our Approach and Model

When it comes to our valuation model, we employ a combination of multiples approach, rather than the infamous Discounted Cash Flow [DCF] model, which many investment banks utilise. To explain, our methodology centres on utilising multiple valuation ratios with an emphasis on forward-looking earnings multiples:

  • EV/Adjusted EBITDA (10% wt);
  • P/E (10% wt);
  • Forward P/E (25% wt);
  • (Forward) PEG (55% wt);

We prioritise forward earnings metrics because stock prices predominantly reflect future earnings expectations rather than past performance. Although, we include trailing ratios with lower weightings in order to provide analytical balance. This is to mitigate the effects of volatility or potential M&A activity.

We believe this approach delivers greater accuracy than a DCF, for the reason that it can account for critical factors such as stock buybacks more accurately. Furthermore, DCFs typically produce significantly higher standard deviations, as minor adjustments to the Weighted Average Cost of Capital [WACC] and/or discount rates can dramatically alter price targets.

In addition to that, DCF models often contain "black box" elements with inadequate explanations regarding the discount rate, WACC determinations, and more crucially, terminal value calculations from perpetual growth rates. On the other hand, our combination of multiples model is more transparent, and more digestible for investors.

Multiples Appendix

  1. EV/Adjusted EBITDA (10% wt)
    • As of TTM to H1'25 results.
    • Market cap data for EV is calculated from multiplying shares outstanding as of latest RNS with the latest share price.
    • Adjusted EBITDA is calculated from: Adjusted EBIT + Depreciation and Amortisation of Non-Current Assets.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Retail (Special Lines) and EU Apparel sectors, and only includes positive EBITDA firms.
  2. P/E (10% wt)
    • As of TTM to H1'25 results.
    • EPS is calculated from adjusted profit after tax, divided by diluted shares outstanding as of latest earnings RNS.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Retail (Special Lines) and EU Apparel sectors, and only includes money-making firms.
  3. FP/E (25% wt)
    • Usually calculated from using weighted forward projections if current period falls between fiscal years.
    • For example, if the current TTM is to H1'25, the calculation will be: (FY25 projected EPS - H1'25 EPS) + (FY26 projected EPS/100 x historical weight of H1 EPS on FY EPS).
    • EPS is calculated from FTM adjusted profit after tax, divided by projected diluted shares outstanding.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Retail (Special Lines) and EU Apparel sectors.
    • EPS consensus data is provided by Refinitiv.
  4. FPEG (55% wt)
    • First, CAGR is calculated as: (FY27 EPS / TTM EPS) ^ (1 / 2.5 yrs) - 1.
    • Then, FPEG is calculated as: (Current share price / TTM EPS) / CAGR.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Retail (Special Lines) and EU Apparel sectors.
    • EPS consensus data is provided by Refinitiv.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: American Express: Affluent Customer Base Softens Spending Uncertainty
data.description:
The stock of American Express (NYSE:AXP) experiences a -21% discount from its highs and is set to report Q1 earnings on Thursday, where the market expects a YoY revenue rise of 7.13% and an earnings gain of 4.27% based on $16.93 billion revenue and $3.47 EPS. The company is the fourth-largest card network in the world and has also been increasing its market share in the lending business. Although AXP has a strong financial position and a great ability to generate shareholder return due to its high ROE, in this analysis, I will give my reasons why entering the stock is not too attractive right now despite the drawdowns and, therefore, rate the stock as a "hold."

Headwinds of Lower Consumer Spending

With all the uncertainty regarding reciprocal tariffs, consumer expectations surveys have taken effect. For example, there was a considerable drop in the U.S. Michigan Consumer Expectations in April. The sentiment index started the year at a strong level of 76.9 and has dropped considerably to 47.2 as consumers fear what could happen to the economy.

At the same time, the NY Fed 1-Year Consumer Inflation Expectations for March came much higher than expected, at 3.6% vs. the previous 3.1%, reversing a downward trend since July 2022.

In a way, the expectations of higher prices in the future could benefit American Express' discount revenues as consumer spending could be boosted in Q2 as households anticipate purchases that would otherwise be delayed due to the fear that these items would be more expensive in the future due to inflation hits from tariffs in items such as cell phones, handbags, and imported wine, for example.

Although higher inflation and lower GDP growth are likely to shrink consumer spending after Q2, American Express has an affluent consumer base with high credit scores that is likely to be less impacted by a potential recession than competitors. Nonetheless, this doesn't mean that they won't be affected, just less affected than a competitor such as Synchrony Financial (SYF) with much lower credit quality, for example.

AXP Stock During Market Turmoil

American Express's stock has a track record of excellent investment performance. Over the past 10 years, the stock has appreciated annually at 13.98%, including dividend payments. Currently, economic uncertainty has hit the share price, and the stock has dropped to approximately $250, a level that hasn't been seen since September 2024.

In the past 10 years, the shares have experienced several drawdowns: -35% in 2016, -31% in 2022, and the weighty -50% from 2020, which was the most severe one due to American Express' exposure to travel purchases. Currently, the stock faces a drawdown of approximately -21%, which is a technical opportunity based on the past performance that the stock has had in recovering from drawdowns.

High ROE and Tailwinds From Diversification And FX

One of American Express' most appealing characteristics is its high return on equity of 34.3%, which is considerably higher than the industry and demonstrates its efficiency in generating shareholder returns. At the same time, the company has a track record of offering appealing dividend hikes, with the most recent being 17.1%, bringing the dividend yield to 1.2%, a fast recovery from the 0.86% exhibited in January. Also, in 2024, the company spent more than $6 billion in share repurchases, which is almost three times the amount paid in dividends.

FY 2024 FY 2014 CAGR
Net Interest Income $15,543 $5,472 11%
Non-Interest Income $50,406 $27,972 6%
Split 76 / 24 84 / 16 -

Source: Seeking Alpha

Another characteristic is that American Express has been growing substantially its lending business, making the revenue contributors more diversified than before. Although the network business and commission fees remain the major top-line contributors, net interest income has grown at almost twice the rate of non-interest income.

For the US, the trade-off of reducing the trade deficit is decreasing its capital account. Therefore, in the long term, this could mean that the rest of the world invests less in the United States, and therefore, there is less demand for the US dollar. 22% of American Express revenues come from outside of the United States, and with the dollar depreciating against foreign currencies measured by the DXY, this could be a tailwind for American Express' financial results.

American Express Shares Remain Fairly Valued

In my previous analysis of American Express in September, I mentioned how the shares were fairly valued based on an intrinsic value model and where the PE of 19.3x was trading compared to its historical average. Seven months later, the valuation hasn't changed, and the shares remain fairly valued.

First, at a PE of 18.23x, the stock sits close to its historical average of 19.44x, and although the multiple has contracted from the 23.26x experienced in January, the drawdown wasn't enough to make the PE ratio undervalued compared to its past. At the same time, a discounted earnings model that takes into account the next three years' analysts' estimates suggests that the fair value of American Express should be $256.23, which is a difference of less than one percent compared to the Monday close price of $255.38.

Takeaway & Why A "Hold" Rating

Although history demonstrates that American Express has a track record of recovering from large drawdowns, the stock valuation isn't undervalued enough to consider an obvious buy on the stock. The company might not be as affected as its competitors as most of its customers are affluent and have high credit scores, and they might not reduce their discretionary spending at the same level as the average household. At the same time, with a fiscal policy that aims to increase the current account, the trade-off of a higher capital account might reduce the demand for the US dollar in the long term, potentially favoring currency translations on financial statements. Taking into account the robustness of the business but comparing the opportunity costs of buying a fairly valued stock with most of the market being fundamentally on sale, I will reiterate my "hold" rating on the shares of American Express.

data.title: KNG Potentially Safer Than JEPI
data.description:
Introduction

Regular readers of mine will know that I have been an evangelist for the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (NYSEARCA:JEPI), most recently issuing it a strong buy recently, mostly because its holdings are value-oriented, and are likely to be less volatile than the S&P 500 more broadly. I praised it for its low allocation to tech firms, and its general tendency toward prudence from management regarding the short side of the options selling the fund does to raise income.

Enter the FT Vest S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats Target Income ETF (BATS:KNG), which promises to be very defensive. Its goal is to own the "dividend aristocrats" of the S&P 500 and sell options against them (individual stocks, not an index) to generate income 8% over the S&P 500's dividend.

Before we get further into the article, it's important to note why I bring up JEPI here. JEPI is the "standard" of derivative income ETFs, and has been both the gold standard of the category in AUM and in my ratings. If KNG can stack up to it from a strategy perspective, that would be a very good look for KNG and could make it a recommendation over JEPI depending on the investor and their goal.

In today's market, where's some funds are finding it hard to stand out, it's not enough to have a compelling strategy on its own, it has to be competitive with what we currently have as well.

Performance Comparison

Over the last few years, we've seen the fund perform admirably against both JEPI and the long-only ProShares S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (NOBL), which shows us how the fund should've performed without its options selling component. We can see that the elevated income only cost returns slightly.

Speaking of income, let's take a look at the aristocrats + 8% yield that KNG is attempting to create and how it stacks up to these two other funds. After all, while NOBL is not an income fund per se, JEPI is. JEPI actively sells calls against the S&P 500 index, not individual stocks like KNG does (more on that in the next section).

That being said, we've seen that since 2024, KNG has been raising its dividend to its current high of 8.5%. Compare this to JEPI's sub-8%, although during 2022 and 2023 it was closer to 12%, and you have a fund that is very competitive from a current yield standpoint.

KNG vs. JEPI Holdings

The two funds hold a much smaller pool of stocks than the S&P 500 (just an assumed base equity position in a portfolio), with JEPI holding around 115 and KNG holding 70.

Here are their top holdings head-to-head. Note that both funds tend to aim for equal weighting.

After looking at the sector split, I believe that KNG is more conservative than JEPI. KNG has a far lower exposure to the highest volatility sectors (those with firms that have a harder time becoming dividend aristocrats due to their age or volatile financial picture) like technology.

While JEPI is low on tech compared to the S&P 500, it's high on tech compared to KNG.

KNG vs. JEPI Derivatives

One of the major differences between these two funds is the way they sell options. KNG sells individual options, while JEPI utilizes ELNs.

KNG sells options on individual stocks in its portfolio, much closer to DIVO's strategy. The advantage of this strategy is that it allows more flexibility from the managers, where they can cut back on being short certain stocks (or avoid it all together) if they believe there is more upside to be had.

On the other hand, JEPI sells the S&P 500 index short via in-house products made by another JPMorgan team. This means that the fund doesn't incur much counter-party risk (or rather, that it matches the issuer risk). If you want to read more about JEPI's ELNs specifically, I covered them in far more detail here.

Here are the major comparisons I believe investors should consider between these two strategies:

Single Stock Options ELNs
More flexibility for stock upside. Holdings are not directly capped.
Stability of income. Higher income overall.
Long & short the same stocks. Long & short different stocks.
Income is RoC. Income is NII.

Don't worry, I'll discuss the last part in the next section.

KNG vs. JEPI Dividends & Taxes

From a dividend standpoint, we see a lot more variance in JEPI, since it derives its income from the S&P 500 index. KNG derives its income from the individual names that it owns, which are less volatile than the index (and less volatile than JEPI's holdings, which are themselves less volatile than the S&P 500 as well), meaning that the variance in its dividend income is much lower.

While this does generally mean less income overall, especially in high-volatility environments like we're in currently, it does mean that income for KNG is more predictable.

From a tax perspective, I've made the argument in the past that JEPI is not as tax-efficient as it could be because of its use of ELNs, which are tools that reduce some risk JEPI would gain from its short position, but also introduce risks such as tax inefficiencies.

Here is where we're bringing back that chart from the previous section:

When looking at the two funds, they distribute income differently. Because JEPI uses ELNs, I wrote that its income is primarily "NII," or "net investment income." These distributions are considered ordinary income in the year you received them for tax purposes. On the other hand, KNG's income is primarily "RoC" or "return-of-capital," which is tax deferred.

RoC reduces an investor's cost-basis, and taxes are realized as capital gains when the shares are sold. This allows investors to defer their taxes, and realize the gains when they want, despite the income hitting their account monthly. This feature is very handy for investors needing to generate income outside of tax-advantaged accounts.

Note that if your cost basis hits zero, future distributions are categories as long-term capital gains in the year you received the distribution.

Because KNG's distributions are RoC and JEPI's are largely NII, this makes JEPI inefficient from a tax perspective, while KNG is very efficient. This could be a large factor in choosing between these two ETFs for tax-sensitive investors.

Fees

There are only a few other aspects that should be pointed out here. Namely, there are investors who are very fee-sensitive. I am not one of them, and routinely advocate high fee funds if I believe that fee is justified. I feel that way about both of these funds, and believe that they earn their fee. That being said, it is of note that JEPI's fee is below market average, while KNG's is above.

For reference, the average for the category is 0.5%.

Suitability

I view KNG is more conservative than JEPI despite its smaller holdings. While there are fewer of them in KNG, they are broadly from more defensive sectors and from companies with very long histories of raising dividends (25 years minimum!) compared to JEPI's already moderate holdings.

For the record, I am still recommending JEPI to all investors. In the past, I have recommended:

...JEPI could be considered for up to 30% of an aggressive investor's portfolio.

However, conservative and moderate investors would be better served by diversifying some of their JEPI position into KNG, as it allows for a defensive and conservative tilt on the JEPI strategy.

Aggressive investors who are tax-sensitive should consider KNG over JEPI for its tax-efficient nature, but also should consider S&P 500 covered call funds outright, like SPYI.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I still believe that the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF is a great pick for riding out our current market volatility, and still believe it to be more prudent than holding the S&P 500 outright.

However, the FT Vest S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats Target Income ETF offers investors access to a more conservative strategy than JEPI from an equity holdings perspective, one which only owns 25+ year dividend payers (and growers), and sells calls in a more tax-efficient manner than JEPI.

This gives investors options, and provides a potentially safer choice for their equity premium exposure in KNG, and opens the discussion to adding KNG exposure to an income portfolio, or diversifying from a JEPI/SPYI allocation to add in more quality.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Dollar On The Ledge - Is A Shove Coming?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

The US dollar is on the ledge. The only question now: Is a shove coming?

Take a look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day showing the ICE US Dollar Index (DXY) from a long-term perspective. It’s not pretty.

DXY just knifed through 100 to the downside. It’s now testing key support that dates back the last few years. Moreover, its year-to-date loss of around 6% puts the greenback on track for its worst annual performance since 2017 (though yes, there’s plenty of time until New Year’s Eve!)

I talked about the main reasons why the dollar is diving in my April 11 column here. But even though they’re more widely known and accepted now... and even though selling has been very aggressive since January... I haven’t seen a key reversal or massive flush on the shorter-term chart (below).

That means it’s (still) hard to lean against the trend as a trader. And if you’re a longer-term investor worried about foreign dumping of US assets hurting stocks and bonds? Well, you’re not seeing much that's encouraging here... yet. Even with investor sentiment in the dumpster and contrarian plays looking more tempting because of it!

Bottom line: It’s probably best to hold your fire before getting aggressive to the long side.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Twin Disc Shares Hammered By Trade Uncertainties And Nervous Investors
data.description:
The word "uncertainty" is likely to feature prominently not just in this article, but in many (if not most) articles these days about manufacturing companies with meaningful international exposure. The market being what it is, uncertainty often means selling out of riskier positions and seeking out safer havens until things calm down. That's certainly been the case for Twin Disc (NASDAQ:TWIN). Although the business has held up well so far, and management's commentary with fiscal second quarter earnings (reported early February) was generally positive, investors are clearly worried about how tariff-driven trade wars and slower global economy growth may impact this small manufacturer of marine and heavy duty power transmission equipment.

Shares are down about 40% since my last update, far worse than the typical industrial, and worse than not only rival manufacturers, but also major customers across marine and heavy-duty markets (where the declines have been more on the order of 5% to 15%). It's tempting to see this sell-off as an opportunity to benefit from a market overreaction, but with so much uncertainty in its end-markets and with global trade policy, I'm reluctant to actively encourage stepping up right now.

Where Is Demand Heading? Nobody Really Seems To Know, But Probably Not "Up" Right Away

As I said above, caution and uncertainty are common words to see and hear these days, as companies try to navigate a new global economic landscape that seems to change every couple of days. It's arguably worse for Twin Disc, given that the products the company sells typically end up in high-value equipment where purchasing decisions are driven by rates and economic outlooks (as opposed to consumables that are more driven by current usage volumes).

Twin Disc's Marine business (close to two-thirds of revenue in fiscal Q2'25) is split between commercial craft (mostly tugs, ferries, offshore supply vessels and other workboats), pleasure craft (yachts), and patrol boats.

The outlook for patrol boats is still healthy, as governments (particularly in Asia) continue to spend on upgraded and expanded fleets, but it is not implausible to me that Twin Disc could face increased scrutiny and competition if governments decide to respond to U.S. trade policies by minimizing their purchases from U.S.-based companies.

Other marine categories are more mixed. Most of the major yacht companies, including Beneteau (OTCPK:BTEAF), Ferretti (9638.HK), and Sanlorenzo (OTCPK:SNLRF), sound fairly confident about the longer-term outlook for their luxury products and are going into 2025 with healthy backlogs, but almost all have sounded notes of caution on issues like the European economy and the potential impact of tariffs.

As far as the commercial market goes, economic uncertainty, relatively high rates, and a lack of shipyard capacity is discouraging newbuild activity in most areas, and current energy prices certainly don't help incentivize new-builds for markets like offshore supply vessels.

Looking at Twin Disc's non-marine markets, the airport, rescue, and fire fighting segment still looks pretty healthy, and with demand driven by municipalities, there is less risk here tied to economic uncertainty (particularly as higher housing prices are working their way through into high property taxes and better-supported municipal budgets). I still see a risk of plateauing orders after a period of strength, but Oshkosh (OSK) and REV Group (REVG) seem to be doing relatively well.

I'm less bullish on other markets like agriculture, construction, and recycling/waste handling equipment, as well as fracking. Higher rates, lower commodity prices, and trade wars are not good for U.S. demand for ag equipment, and I likewise see a real risk that ongoing economic uncertainty will weigh on construction activity and new equipment demand. As far as fracking is concerned, activity was already soft back in late 2024 and weakening oil prices are making several U.S. basins less competitive, further limiting near-term capex spending.

Self-Help Is Badly Needed, And I'm Less Confident Here

As I've said in other recent articles on Twin Disc, one of my biggest concerns with recommending the stock is that the company has a relatively poor track record when it comes to sustained revenue growth and margin leverage. The company made strategic moves that should help this - including acquiring more high-value products (the Veth deal), further diversifying the business through M&A, and looking to focus on higher-value systems - but "should" only gets you so far.

Looking back at the fiscal second quarter, gross margin was down about four points year over year and more than two points sequentially, and even adjusting for some write-downs still leaves a two-point year-over-year decline and a slight sequential decline. It's a little better at the EBITDA and operating income lines, with year-over-year margin improvements on adjusted numbers, but management themselves acknowledged that they need to drive more operational efficiencies to improve margins.

Tariffs are going to be a looming risk for the near term. Although Twin Disc has a diverse sales base (over 40% of sales to Europe, nearly one-quarter to Asia, and about one-quarter in North America) and a diverse manufacturing footprint (with plants in the U.S. and several European countries), retaliatory tariffs remain a risk, as do higher input costs in the United States. Management has not quantified this risk, and I'm not sure how they could, given the frequent changes in tariff policy, but none of this is going to be beneficial to margins in the near term.

I just don't have the confidence that self-help can offset these near-term pressures. I like the company's leverage to higher-value systems for yachts, but I'm worried that that market is slowing. Likewise, I like the long-term leverage to electric and hybrid systems for marine customers, but I don't expect that to be a major driver over the next 12-24 months.

The Outlook

The first two quarters of fiscal 2025 have been about what I expected to see, so I'm reluctant to make major changes to my model at this point. That said, tariffs and macro uncertainties do appear to be having a negative impact on global economic activity, and I've modestly trimmed my revenue expectations (by about 2% to 3%) as a result.

I've likewise pulled back my margin expectations, as I think it will be harder to generate gross margin leverage in the current environment and I think that will weigh on EBITDA and operating margin leverage. I'm still expecting EBITDA margins in the 8%-9% range, but I'm not willing to model double-digit margins in the near term without more evidence of a sustained improvement.

All told, I'm still looking for long-term revenue growth around 4%, as I do like the company's efforts to diversify its business into adjacent products/markets (like the recent acquisition of Kobelt) and I think the yacht business is fundamentally a good one for a company like Twin Disc. I'm still skeptical that Twin Disc can get free cash flow margins much above 3% to 5% on a sustained basis, and even the low end of that range is a marked improvement over its long-term averages.

The market expects even less from Twin Disc, as both my discounted cash flow and margin/return-driven EV/EBITDA (using a 6.5x forward multiple) valuation approaches still get me to fair values in the double-digits. To that end, I'd note that Twin Disc is trading at around 4x my 12-month EBITDA estimate - which is about as bad as it gets for manufacturing companies that aren't distressed, but here again I note that for small, uncovered companies, market sell-offs can drive extremely low multiples.

The Bottom Line

The biggest issue I have with recommending Twin Disc now is that there are many higher-quality companies that have also sold off in recent weeks/months and offer risk/reward profiles that depend less upon significant self-improvement relative to historical performance. Whether or not the company can hit its own self-improvements remains a key unknown, and this newly heightened uncertainty around U.S. policies and the global economy doesn't help.

This may well prove to be an excellent buying opportunity, but I still see risks that key end-markets like yachts, commercial marine vessels, and land-based heavy-duty machinery could weaken further, and that the company's margin structure may come under further pressure. It's certainly a candidate for a watch list, but I need to see more data on where margins and key end-markets are heading before getting more bullish.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
